programs.name: HUSKY C
programs.what_it_does: HUSKY C provides Medicaid health coverage for low-income seniors aged 65 and older and individuals who are blind or disabled in Connecticut. It covers long-term care services including nursing home care, home health services, and supports to help frail elders remain at home.[1][2]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut residents aged 65+ or blind/disabled with income under nursing home cost limits (2026) and assets under $1,600 for single applicants requiring nursing home level of care.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Full Medicaid coverage including long-term care; no premium for eligible; asset limit $1,600 single (2026).[1]

programs.name: Connecticut Home Care Program for Elders (CHCPE)
programs.what_it_does: CHCPE is a Medicaid home and community-based services (HCBS) waiver that provides medical and non-medical support services to help frail elders avoid nursing home placement. Services include personal care, adult day care, homemaker assistance, and other supports based on individual needs assessment.[2][7]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut residents aged 65+ at risk of nursing home placement (needing help with bathing, dressing, eating, medications, toileting) who meet financial eligibility criteria.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Comprehensive home care services to delay institutionalization; limited enrollment slots may have waitlists.[2]

programs.name: Connecticut PACE Programs
programs.what_it_does: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE) provides comprehensive medical and social services to eligible frail elders through adult day health centers and in-home care. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid covered services plus additional supports to enable participants to live safely in the community.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut residents aged 55+ (typically 65+) certified as needing nursing home level care but able to live safely in community with PACE services; must live in a PACE service area.
programs.benefit_value: Full capitated coverage of all needed services; no cost for full Medicaid/Medicare dual eligibles.

programs.name: Connecticut Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: These programs help pay Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments for eligible low-income seniors. QMB covers full Part B premium ($185.00/mo 2026) plus cost-sharing; SLMB covers Part B premium; QI covers Part B for QI-1 eligibles.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut Medicare beneficiaries with limited income/assets: QMB ≤$1,325/mo single (2026), SLMB ≤$1,585/mo, QI ≤$1,775/mo; assets typically ≤$9,660 single.[3]
programs.benefit_value: QMB: $185/mo Part B + cost-sharing; SLMB: $185/mo premium; QI: $185/mo premium; applied via DSS.[3]

programs.name: Connecticut SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: SNAP provides monthly benefits via EBT card to purchase healthy food at approved retailers. It helps low-income seniors afford nutritious meals to maintain health and independence.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut seniors 60+ with income ≤130% FPL ($1,634/mo single 2026) and assets ≤$4,500; higher deductions for elders.
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/mo per eligible senior household (2026 max $291 single); broad grocery purchase eligibility.

programs.name: Connecticut Energy Assistance Program (CEAP)
programs.what_it_does: CEAP provides cash grants to help eligible households pay heating costs during winter months. It also offers crisis assistance for fuel emergencies and connects to weatherization services.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut households with income ≤60% state median ($44,100 single 2026) including seniors; priority for 60+ and disabled.
programs.benefit_value: Average $700-1,000 winter benefit per household; crisis grants up to $1,000; annual program Nov-Apr.

programs.name: Connecticut Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: WAP provides no-cost energy efficiency upgrades including insulation, air sealing, furnace repairs/replacements to reduce energy costs. Services delivered through community action agencies statewide.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Connecticut households ≤200% FPL ($30,120 single 2026) with elderly, disabled, or children; income priority.
programs.benefit_value: Free comprehensive home energy upgrades valued $5,000-15,000 per home; reduces utility bills 20-30%.

programs.name: Connecticut Health Insurance Assistance Program (HICAP/SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: HICAP provides free, unbiased counseling on Medicare, Medigap, Medicare Advantage, Part D, and related health insurance issues. Volunteer counselors serve all 169 CT towns via phone and in-person.[3]
programs.who_its_for: All Connecticut Medicare beneficiaries, families, and caregivers seeking health coverage information and assistance.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; annual open enrollment assistance; helps save thousands on coverage decisions.

programs.name: Connecticut Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Meals on Wheels delivers nutritious hot meals to homebound seniors through 28 local Aging & Disability Resource Centers. Program coordinates with 100+ congregate meal sites serving 1.2M meals annually.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut residents 60+ who are homebound and unable to prepare meals; modest suggested contribution.
programs.benefit_value: 5-7 meals/week delivered; congregate meals; nutrition counseling; serves 10,000+ seniors daily.

programs.name: Connecticut Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite grants, caregiver training, support groups, and counseling to family caregivers of elders. Administered through ADRCs with Medicaid waiver caregiver stipends up to $35/hour.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of Connecticut residents 60+ or 18-59 with Alzheimer’s; financial eligibility for paid respite.
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $1,600/year; training; Medicaid paid caregiver option up to 40 hrs/week.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: SCSEP provides part-time community service work training and employment assistance to unemployed low-income seniors. Pays minimum wage for 20 hrs/week work-based training leading to unsubsidized jobs.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed Connecticut residents 55+ with income ≤125% FPL ($18,825 single 2026); priority for veterans, disabled.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage work training 20 hrs/week; employment placement; serves 1,000+ CT seniors annually.

programs.name: Connecticut Legal Assistance Program for Elders
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal services to low-income seniors on elder law issues including housing, benefits, consumer protection, long-term care, and abuse prevention. Operates statewide with 5 offices.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut residents 60+ with income ≤125% FPL ($18,825 single 2026) facing legal issues affecting independence.
programs.benefit_value: Free legal representation valued $250+/hr; served 2,500+ CT seniors in 2025; priority case acceptance.

programs.name: Connecticut Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Ombudsman volunteers advocate for residents' rights in CT nursing homes, assisted living, and other long-term care facilities. Investigates complaints, provides education, and ensures quality of care.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut long-term care residents and families needing advocacy; serves all 300+ licensed facilities statewide.
programs.benefit_value: Free confidential advocacy; resolved 85% of 4,000+ annual complaints; prevents unnecessary institutionalization.

programs.name: Congregate Housing Services Program (CHSP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides supportive housing services including meals, housekeeping, personal care, and social activities in congregate settings for older adults. It helps participants live independently in community-based housing while receiving necessary supports.[2][3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income adults aged 62 and older who are frail or at risk of nursing home placement and meet income eligibility.
programs.whats_unique: Connecticut's state-administered program under Medicaid waiver authority, focusing on service-enriched congregate housing unique to the state's long-term care rebalancing strategy.[1][3]
programs.benefit_value: Comprehensive services including up to 10 meals/week, personal care, and transportation; state-funded beyond federal standards.

programs.name: Connecticut Private Pay Assisted Living Pilot Program
programs.what_it_does: Subsidizes private-pay nursing care costs within assisted living facilities to help seniors afford care without entering costly nursing homes. The state pays a portion of medical expenses, allowing residents to use other income sources for room and board.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut assisted living residents with qualifying medical needs and financial situations, often those with Social Security, pensions, or VA benefits.
programs.whats_unique: Innovative state budget program that directly pays up to $2,360/month for nursing care in assisted living, reducing Medicaid nursing home costs—a model not replicated in most states.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Up to $2,360 per month toward nursing care costs in assisted living.

programs.name: Property Tax Credit for Elderly/Disabled
programs.what_it_does: Offers a state tax credit to reduce property tax burdens for eligible homeowners. It provides direct financial relief based on income and assets.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Homeowners aged 65+ or disabled, with income under state thresholds (e.g., typically under $45,000 for singles).
programs.whats_unique: Connecticut-specific credit program with state-funded rebates tailored to elderly and disabled residents, beyond federal tax provisions.[1][9]
programs.benefit_value: Up to $1,200 annually, depending on income and property taxes paid.

programs.name: Renters’ Rebate for Elderly/Disabled
programs.what_it_does: Provides a rebate credit for rent paid by eligible elderly or disabled renters to offset housing costs. Claimed via state tax filing.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Renters aged 65+ or disabled, meeting income limits similar to property tax credit.
programs.whats_unique: State-funded rebate program exclusive to Connecticut, offering relief analogous to property tax credit but for non-homeowners.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Up to $700 annually based on rent paid and income.

programs.name: Connecticut Statewide Respite Care Program
programs.what_it_does: Funds respite services for caregivers of older adults, including in-home, adult day, or out-of-home care to provide temporary relief. Also includes the Statewide Alzheimer’s Respite Program for dementia caregivers.[1][2]
programs.who_its_for: Caregivers of CT elders 60+ or adults with Alzheimer’s/dementia; income-based eligibility.
programs.whats_unique: Statewide coordinated program with dedicated funding for Alzheimer’s respite, offering up to 30 days out-of-home care—not a universal state offering.[1][2]
programs.benefit_value: Up to $3,500/year per applicant; max 30 days out-of-home respite.

programs.name: CHOICES Program
programs.what_it_does: Delivers health insurance counseling, eligibility screening, and referral for Medicare, Medicaid, and state benefits to seniors and disabled adults. Includes one-stop screening for nearly 20 programs.[3][6]
programs.who_its_for: Connecticut residents aged 60+ or disabled individuals seeking benefits information.
programs.whats_unique: Connecticut's branded statewide counseling and screening service with toll-free access and volunteer network, customized to state programs.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Free counseling and application assistance; connects to thousands in savings via accessed benefits.

programs.name: Money Follows the Person (MFP) Program
programs.what_it_does: Assists institutionalized individuals in transitioning back to community living with housing, services, and supports for up to 12 months. Part of CT's rebalancing long-term care strategy.[1][2][3]
programs.who_its_for: CT Medicaid-eligible adults 65+ or disabled who have been in nursing facilities for 90+ days.
programs.whats_unique: Connecticut's implementation of the federal demo with state-specific projections and town-by-town strategies for long-term care rebalancing.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Transition services and supports valued up to $15,000+ per person.
